coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of flake-shaped nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dot/polyaniline (N-CQD/PANI) nanocomposites via rapid-mixing polymerization and their application as electrode materials in supercapacitors
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Qi
Wang, Hongxing
Liu, Dong
Du, Pengcheng
Liu, Peng
coredata.dc:description: Flake-shaped nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dot/polyaniline (N-CQD/PANI) nanocomposites were prepared as electrode materials in supercapacitors via rapid-mixing chemical oxidative polymerization of aniline in the presence of the nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) containing abundant amino groups, which were synthesized via a one-step microwave pyrolysis with citric acid (CA) as the carbon source and 1,2-ethylenediamine (EDA) as the surface passivation agent. The effect of the synthetic conditions on the morphology, electrical conductivity and electrochemical properties of the resultant nanocomposites was investigated. The electrochemical experiments showed that all the N-CQD/PANI nanocomposites exhibited higher specific capacitances than the PANI one, and the N-CQD/PANI-3 one, prepared at an aniline concentration of 0.02mol/L with a feeding ratio of N-CQDs to aniline of 10wt%, possessed the highest electrical conductivity, specific capacitance, rate capability and cycling stability.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Supercapacitors
Electrode materials
Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots
Polyaniline
Rapid-mixing polymerization

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and characterization of N-CQD/PANI nanocomposites

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the N-CQD/PANI nanocomposites.
TEM images of the N-CQTs (a), PANI (b), N-CQD/PANI-1 (c), N-CQD/PANI-2 (d), N-CQD/PANI-3 (e), N-CQD/PANI-4 (f), N-CQD/PANI-5 (g), N-CQD/PANI-6 (h), and N-CQD/PANI-7 (i).
FT-IR spectra of the PANI and N-CQD/PANI-3 nanocomposite.
GVD curves of the N-CQD/PANI nanocomposites prepared with different feeding ratios of N-QCDs (a) and different aniline concentrations (b), at a current density of 1A/g.
GCD curves of the PANI (a), N-CQD/PANI-1 (b), N-CQD/PANI-2 (c), N-CQD/PANI-3 (d), N-CQD/PANI-4 (e) at different current densities, and their specific capacitances (f).
CV curves of the PANI (a), N-CQD/PANI-1 (b), N-CQD/PANI-2 (c), N-CQD/PANI-3 (d), N-CQD/PANI-4 (e) at different scan rates, and their specific capacitances (f).
Nyquist plots of PANI and the N-CQD/PANI nanocomposite electrodes (Inset: magnified high-frequency regions).
Capacitance retention of the N-CQD/PANI nanocomposite electrodes and PANI electrode at 100mV/s.
Polymerizing conditions for the N-CQD/PANI nanocomposites and their electrical conductivity and capacitance properties.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Calculated from GCD tests at a current density of 1.0A/g.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Calculated from CV tests at a scan rate of 5.0mV/s.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of flake-shaped nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dot/polyaniline (N-CQD/PANI) nanocomposites via rapid-mixing polymerization and their application as electrode materials in supercapacitors

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Applied Organic Chemistry and Key Laboratory of Nonferrous Metal Chemistry and Resources Utilization of Gansu Province, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 730000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Applied Organic Chemistry and Key Laboratory of Nonferrous Metal Chemistry and Resources Utilization of Gansu Province
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Flake-shaped nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dot/polyaniline (N-CQD/PANI) nanocomposites were prepared as electrode materials in supercapacitors via rapid-mixing chemical oxidative polymerization of aniline in the presence of the nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) containing abundant amino groups, which were synthesized via a one-step microwave pyrolysis with citric acid (CA) as the carbon source and 1,2-ethylenediamine (EDA) as the surface passivation agent. The effect of the synthetic conditions on the morphology, electrical conductivity and electrochemical properties of the resultant nanocomposites was investigated. The electrochemical experiments showed that all the N-CQD/PANI nanocomposites exhibited higher specific capacitances than the PANI one, and the N-CQD/PANI-3 one, prepared at an aniline concentration of 0.02mol/L with a feeding ratio of N-CQDs to aniline of 10wt%, possessed the highest electrical conductivity, specific capacitance, rate capability and cycling stability.

ce:section.ce:para: Supercapacitors have attracted more and more attention due to their unique advantages in comparison to other electrochemical energy storage devices, such as high power density, excellent cycling stability, fast charging–discharging rate, operational security, etc [1,2]. The conductive polymers are one of the main kinds of electrode materials for pseudocapacitors, which could store electrical energy via the faradic charge transfer reactions or reversible redox reaction with high specific capacitance. Unfortunately, their slow response and poor stability restrict their practical applications [1,2]. In order to improve the charging–discharging rate, specific capacitance and cycling life, the composite electrode materials have been widely designed with conductive substrates and pseudocapacitive materials, integrating the merits of electrochemical double-layer capacitors (EDLCs) and pseudo-supercapacitors [3].

Among the conductive polymers, polyaniline (PANI) has been considered as one of the most promising candidate electrode materials owing to its excellent environmental and chemistry stability, fast redox rate, high electric conductibility and specific pseudocapacitance, low cost and facile synthesis [4,5], especially in nanoscale [6]. In the last decades, PANI-based nanocomposites have been intensely developed as electrode materials for supercapacitors to improve the cycling stability, mainly composing with the carbon nanomaterials, such as carbon nanotubes [7] and graphene [7,8].

As a rising star of carbon nanomaterials, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been widely investigated in various fields such as sensing [9], bioimaging [10], photocatalysis [11], photovoltaics and electrochemical devices [12], due to their unique properties in comparison to other carbon nanostructures (fullerene, graphene, and carbon nanotubes), such as strong fluorescence, high photostability, chemical inertness and low toxicity. For the supercapacitor applications, the CQDs-based nanocomposites have been designed as electrode materials, with reduced graphene oxide (rGO) [13], lamellar Ni(OH)2 [14], and polypyrrole [15]. Most recently, Zhao and Xie developed a flexible supercapacitor based on carbon quantum dots-polyaniline/carbon fibers (CQDs-PANI/CFs) via a by directly photoelectrodepositing the CQDs and PANI on the surface of CFs [16]. The results showed that the incorporation of CQDs into PANI could improve the conductivity and alleviate the volume change of the CQDs-PANI electrode during the charge/discharge process.

In the present work, the flake-shaped nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dot/polyaniline (N-CQD/PANI) nanocomposites were synthesized via rapid-mixing chemical oxidative polymerization of aniline in presence of the nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) prepared via a one-step microwave pyrolysis technique. Owing to the plentiful functional groups on the surfaces of the N-CQDs, such as amino and carboxyl groups, the PANI chains are expected to be covalently grafted from the N-CQDs, or adsorbed via electrostatic interaction or hydrogen bond, as shown in Scheme 1 . Such structure is expected to enhance the comprehensive electrochemical performance of PANI, including specific capacitance and cycling stability.

Citric acid anhydrous (CA, 99.5%) was bought from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Ethylenediamine (EDA, 99%) was purchased from Rionlon Bohua (Tianjin) Pharmaceutical Chemical Co. Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Aniline (Shanghai Zhongqin Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd., Shanghai, China) was freshly distilled under reduced pressure before use. Ammonium peroxydisulfate (APS) was obtained from Tianjin Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd, China). Other chemicals were analytical grade and used without further purification. Doubly deionized water was used throughout.

The nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) with plentiful amino groups were synthesized via a one-step microwave pyrolysis approach [18]. Briefly, 2.0g (10.41mmol) of CA and 0.9358g (15.57mmol) of EDA were dissolved in 20mL of water. Then the solution was placed in a domestic microwave oven (700W) and powered for 4min. After cooling to room temperature, the N-CQDs were separated by dialysis (MWCO of 1000) with water for 3days, and lyophilized finally.

The N-CQD/PANI nanocomposites were synthesized via a facile rapid-mixing chemical oxidative polymerization of aniline with APS as oxidant (molar ratio between aniline and APS of 1:1.25) in presence of N-CQDs [19]. Typically, the N-CQDs were dispersed into 30mL water by ultrasonication for 20min. Then a certain amount of aniline was added and stirred for 1h. After that, 30mL 1.0M sulfuric acid solution containing APS was rapidly mixed and the mixture was vigorously shaken for 1min (Table 1 ). After 24h standing in ice-water bathing, the products were collected by filtration and washed with ethanol for several times. Finally, the resulting products were dried at 45°C under vacuum for 12h.
For comparison, PANI sample without N-CQDs was also synthesized with the similar procedure as the N-CQD/PANI-3 nanocomposite.

The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded with a Bruker IFS 66v/s infrared spectrometer in 400–4000cm−1 with a resolution of 4cm−1, using the potassium bromide (KBr) pellet technique.

EM-1230 transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL, Tokyo, Japan) was applied to study the morphologies of the samples. The samples were dispersed in ethanol with ultrasonication and then dipped onto the copper mesh, and dried at room temperature.

The electrical conductivities of the sample powders were measured using a RTS-2 four-point probe conductivity tester (Guangzhou four-point probe Technology Co., Ltd, Guangdong, China) at ambient temperature. The pellet was obtained by subjecting the powder to a pressure of about 20MPa. The value is an average of at least three measurements.

The electrochemical performance of the nanocomposites was evaluated with a three electrode system on a CHI660E electrochemical workstation (CHI, Shanghai) at the potential range of −0.2–0.8V in 1.0M H2SO4 aqueous solution. The working electrodes were prepared by coating a paste mixture (nanocomposites, carbon black and polyvinylidenefluoride (PVDF) with a mass ratio of 80:10:10 in dimethylformamide (DMF)) on a carbon plate with an area of 1cm2 and dried at 45°C for 12h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of N-CQD/PANI nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: N stretching vibration of quinoid and benzenoid structure, and 1128cm−1 of the protonated −NH+ =structure which is correlated with both electron delocalization and N-H+⋯N interchain hydrogen bonding [21]. However, the CC stretching band redshifted to 1554 and 1477cm−1 respectively in the N-CQD/PANI nanocomposite, due to the electrostatic interaction, hydrogen bond, as well as π–π interaction between PANI and N-CQDs [22,23]. Furthermore, the absorbance of the protonated −NH+ =group redshifted to 1122cm−1 in the N-CQD/PANI-3 nanocomposite, also revealing the formation of hydrogen bonding between the N-CQDs and PANI. These strong interactions (as shown in Scheme 1) maybe beneficial to the cycling stability of the nanocomposites during the long–term cycle test [24].

The effect of the polymerizing conditions on the electrical conductivity and specific capacitances (Cm from GCD tests at a current density of 1.0A/g (Fig. 4 ) and Cv from CV tests at a scan rate of 5.0mV/s) of the N-CQD/PANI nanocomposites was summarized in Table 1. All the N-CQD/PANI nanocomposites exhibited higher specific capacitances than the PANI one, and the N-CQD/PANI-3 one, prepared at an aniline concentration of 0.02mol/L with a feeding ratio of N-CQDs to aniline of 10wt%, possessed the highest electrical conductivity and specific capacitance. In order to explain the effect of the N-CQDs feeding ratio on the electrochemical property of the nanocomposites, the morphologies of the N-CQD/PANI nanocomposites were characterized with TEM technique. Interestingly, these N-CQD/PANI nanocomposites showed the flake-shaped morphology, different from the PANI prepared without N-CQDs, which showed serious aggregation of the nanoscaled PANI spheres or flakes (Fig. 1b). It might be due to effect of N-CQDs on the nucleation mode of PANI, similar as reported previously [25]. The N-CQD/PANI-1 nanocomposite showed a transition state from nanoparticles to flakes ((Fig. 1c). With feeding ratio of N-CQDs to aniline above 7.5wt%, the products were flake-shaped. The N-CQD/PANI-2 nanocomposite with feeding ratio of N-CQDs to aniline of 7.5wt% had a smaller size in nanoscaled, while the N-CQD/PANI-3 and N-CQD/PANI-4 nanocomposites showed larger size in submicrometers. And the N-CQD/PANI-4 nanocomposite with high feeding ratio of N-CQDs showed more serious agglomeration than the N-CQD/PANI-3 one. Moreover, with the same feeding ratio of N-CQDs/aniline of 10%, the composite flakes became smaller with increasing the aniline concentration in the rapid-mixing chemical oxidative polymerization. The analysis indicated that the nanocomposite flakes with appropriate size and less agglomeration might be a promising candidate as electrode material for supercapacitors.

In the GCD curves at different current densities of 1.0–10.0A/g (Fig. 5 (a-e)), all the N-CQD/PANI nanocomposite electrodes gave a symmetrically triangle-shaped curve as well as the PANI electrode, demonstrating their good electrochemical reversibility. Different from the PANI electrode, no obvious IR-drop can be found for the N-CQD/PANI nanocomposite electrodes, demonstrating the outstanding capacitance behavior and the promising charging/discharging performance of the N-CQD/PANI nanocomposite electrodes. At a low current density of 1.0A/g, the specific capacitance (Cm) was 314, 406, 498, or 290F/g for the N-CQD/PANI-1, N-CQD/PANI-2, N-CQD/PANI-3, or N-CQD/PANI-4 nanocomposite electrode, respectively. The data were near 2–3 times high of the PANI electrode (168F/g), indicating that introducing N-CQDs could significantly improve the electrochemical performance of PANI. The rate performance of the N-CQD/PANI nanocomposite electrodes was further evaluated at current density ranging from 1.0 to 10.0A/g. Increasing the current density, the specific capacitance declined due to the internal resistance and polarization effect (Fig. 5(f)) [26]. The capacitance retentions were still 58%, 64%, 70%, and 57% at the highest current density of 10.0A/g for the N-CQD/PANI-1, N-CQD/PANI-2, N-CQD/PANI-3, and N-CQD/PANI-4 nanocomposite electrodes, respectively. All these retention values were much higher than that of the PANI electrode of 29%, demonstrating that the flake-shaped nanocomposite electrodes exhibited better good rate capability. Furthermore, the N-CQD/PANI-3 nanocomposite electrode, which possessed the highest specific capacitance, also showed the most excellent rate capability.

The CV curves of the N-CQD/PANI nanocomposite electrodes at different scan rates of 5.0–100.0mV/s demonstrated the typical CV characteristic of PANI with two pairs of redox peaks: the redox peaks O1/R1 (0.3V) of the redox transitions between leucoemeraldine form and polaronic emeraldine and the redox peaks O2/R2 (0.5V) of the Faradic transformation of emeraldine to pernigraniline [27], which results in the higher Faradic capacitance (Fig. 6 ). Besides, the anodic peaks (O1/O2) shifted positively and the cathodic peaks (R1/R2) shifted negatively with the increment of scan rates, because the high scan rate prevents the accessibility of ions to the electrode [28]. Furthermore, the redox peaks are still retained at a higher scan rate, indicating the good rate capability and electrochemically capacitance behavior of the N-CQD/PANI nanocomposites.

The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) clearly showed that the N-CQD/PANI nanocomposite electrodes had smaller charge transfer resistance (Fig. 7 ). The equivalent series resistance and charges transfer resistance (Rs and Rct) were calculated as 1.18, 0.92, 1.02, 1.74Ω and 1.37, 0.82, 0.67, 1.24Ω for the N-CQD/PANI-1, N-CQD/PANI-2, N-CQD/PANI-3, and N-CQD/PANI-4 nanocomposite electrodes, respectively. Most of them were lower than the data of the PANI sample (1.66 and 1.13Ω), in agreement with the electrical conductivity analysis (Table 1). Both the relatively smaller internal resistance and charge transfer resistance of the N-CQD/PANI-3 nanocomposite electrode contribute to the high rate capability.

The cycling stability curves of the N-CQD/PANI-1, N-CQD/PANI-2, N-CQD/PANI-3, and N-CQD/PANI-4 nanocomposite electrodes were compared with the PANI electrode at a constant density of 10.0A/g, as illustrated in Fig. 8 . All the electrodes showed the similar attenuation trend with the increasing in cycling numbers, especially in the first 1000 cycles. It might be mainly due to the volume expansion and shrinkage of PANI, resulted from the intercalation-deintercalation process of the electrolyte ions during the charge-discharge cycles, while the slower attenuation in the following 1000 cycles should be mainly caused by the hydrolytic degradation of PANI [29]. Compared with the PANI electrode with specific capacitance retention of 25.3% after 2000 cycles, all the four N-CQD/PANI nanocomposite electrodes showed better cycling stability with specific capacitance retention of 29.6%, 33.1%, 36.2% and 30.4% of the N-CQD/PANI-1, N-CQD/PANI-2, N-CQD/PANI-3, and N-CQD/PANI-4 nanocomposite electrodes, respectively. The results indicated that the nanostructured flake-shaped morphology and strong interactions between the N-CQDs and PANI enable a recoverable large volume change during the repeated ion insertion/extraction in PANI leading to the good electrochemical performance [28]. Especially for the flake-shaped N-CQD/PANI-3 nanocomposite, its larger size and less aggregation endowed the most excellent comprehensive electrochemical performance.

For the first time, the flake-shaped nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dot/polyaniline (N-CQD/PANI) nanocomposites were prepared via rapid-mixing chemical oxidative polymerization of aniline in the presence of the nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) with plentiful amino groups, which were synthesized via a one-step microwave pyrolysis with citric acid (CA) as the carbon source and 1,2-ethylenediamine (EDA) as the surface passivation agent. The effect of the synthetic conditions on the morphology, electrical conductivity and electrochemical properties of the resultant nanocomposites was investigated. Owing to the plentiful functional groups on the surfaces of the N-CQDs, such as amino and carboxyl groups, the PANI chains be covalently grafted from the N-CQDs, or adsorbed via electrostatic interaction or hydrogen bond. Therefore, the enhanced electrical conductivity and comprehensive electrochemical performance (including specific capacitance and cycling stability) were obtained. Especially for the flake-shaped N-CQD/PANI-3 nanocomposite, its larger size and less aggregation endowed the most excellent comprehensive electrochemical performance, making it a promising candidate electrode material for high-performance supercapacitors.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Science and Technology Project of Chengguan District, Lanzhou City(Grant No. 2014-2-1).
